entry.title: Re: Professional services. Employee time with Sales price 0.
entry.content:
Hi @kat_kos01 ,

you are describing a common case for a Professional Service customer. The standard procedure for hours that should only post the cost on the project, but not triggering revenue recognition or being available for billing is to maintain them as non-billable. So ideally you plan from the beginning in such cases also a role for those employees and mark them as non billable. In your case you could, instead of the new work package, create additional roles in your existing work package that you mark as non billable and then reduce the planned hours from the billable position and increase on the non-billable. As an alternative you can write off those hours manually during project billing.

The sales price you maintained is not picked up as a sales rate of 0 is not valid. Therefore the system doesn't determine a sales price and takes the actual cost rate as a sales rate.

Best regards,

David

entry.content: It will be assigned to the ledger with local accounts principle, which needs to be assigned at company code level to get statistical depreciation area. So in your case, with 0L using local accounts principle, it will be created here. Accounts principle IFRS should be assigned at ledger level, only area 32 will ever be delivered with IFRS.

entry.title: Help in SAP Build Code
entry.content:
We are currently developing a project using SAP Build Code Fullstack. As part of this initiative, we needed to integrate the schema, services, and applications from a previously developed project in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS).

The migration was successfully completed in terms of file structure and syntax. The views in test mode render correctly and maintain the same structure as in the original BAS project. However, when working in the SAP Build Code environment, we consistently encounter the following issue:

Displayed message:

"Storyboard editor for 'MonitorFacturasBuild' will be displayed without attributes due to size limitations"

Once this message appears, the entire development environment freezes, and we are unable to interact with any files or components. Reloading or exiting the page does not resolve the issue, and the problem persists with every session.

entry.title: Re: Onboarding - Learning integration for Pre-day1 New Hires
entry.content:
@bharathprakas269 

Yes, if you add a fixed value field in the IC job, then it will need to be mapped to a field in LMS. It could be a standard one, i.e., Department, or you can utilize a custom field. The point is that whatever field you use, it will be populated with the same value for ALL onboardees that comes through that IC job by setting a default value in the job > configure fields, and then you use it in APs to assign accordingly.

I am not IC expert, but I have used fixed value fields before, and I don't see why it could not be utilized in the IC job for this integration.

As for the onboarding status, I believe this is managed by adding a transformation configuration in LMS that will ultimately transform 'Active_external_suite' to 'active' during the sync, since LMS looks for active/inactive.

  1. Navigate to Admin Center   Learning Administrator   System Administration   Configuration   System Configuration   CONNECTORS.

  • Search for the Configuration Key “sfuser.field.transform.data.NOTACTIVE.N” and add active_external_suite.

  • Search for the Configuration Key “sfuser.field.transform.data.NOTACTIVE.Y” and add inactive_external_suite.

  • Search for the Configuration Key “sfuser.field.transform.data.SHOPPING_ACCT_TYPE.INTERNAL” and add active_external_suite,inactive_external_suite.

  • Search for the Configuration Key “sfuser.connector.input.file.multiple.format” and make sure you have this configuration available TIMESTAMP_BEFORE_EXTENSION.

  • If it isn't already available, add sfuser.connector.input.file.header.skip.records.count=0 setting in the Connector file.

    Defining this setting to ‘0’ ensures that Learning considers the data imported as user data, and doesn’t skip the second line."

Hope this helps,

Melanie

entry.title: Re: Planning for Work Centers using PPDS
entry.content:
Hello Pruthvee

You should be able to model in SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for Planning and Scheduling (PP/DS) using a combination of setup and processing activities with different resources and calendars.

Step-by-step configuration:

  1. Split the operation into two activities:

    • Activity 1: Setup activity – represents the operator-dependent task.
    • Activity 2: Processing activity – represents the machine-only task.

  2. Assign separate resources and calendars:

    • Operator Resource: Assigned to the Setup activity, with a shift-based calendar (e.g., 08:00–16:00).
    • Machine Resource: Assigned to the Processing activity, with a 24/7 calendar.

  3. Model durations in the routing (BOM + Routing → PDS):

    • Setup duration: e.g., 1 hour.
    • Processing duration: e.g., 10 hours.

  4. Use standard PP/DS heuristics (e.g., /SAPAPO/HEUR_PLAN_001):

    • The heuristic will schedule the setup to start only when the operator resource is available.
    • Once the setup is complete, the processing will continue based on machine availability (24/7), even outside operator shifts.

Alternative (single operation): Use a secondary resource for the operator:

  • Keep one operation.
  • Assign the machine as the main resource.
  • Assign the operator as a secondary resource, required only during setup time.
  • The system will check availability of both resources for setup and continue with machine processing afterward.

Validation:

  • Use transaction /SAPAPO/CDPS0 (DS Planning Board) to visualize scheduling.
  • Ensure calendars are correctly assigned to each resource.
  • Run PP/DS heuristics and confirm that the start of production aligns with operator availability, but execution continues based on the machine calendar.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: ONB project plan
entry.content:
Hello Tia 

Yes, SAP provides official project guidance for implementing SuccessFactors Onboarding (ONB), Offboarding (OFB), and Crossboarding, but there is no single public-facing document that provides a complete project plan in downloadable format. However, you can access project methodology, deliverables, and best practices via the following official SAP tools and portals.

1. SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer

This is the primary SAP portal for implementation methodology across all SAP solutions, including SuccessFactors modules.

Access steps:

  • Go to: https://go.support.sap.com/roadmapviewer/
  • Select:
    • Line of Business: Human Experience Management (HXM)
    • Roadmap: "SAP SuccessFactors HXM Suite Implementation" or "SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding Implementation"

What you'll find:

  • Phase-wise activities (Prepare, Explore, Realize, etc.)
  • Detailed tasks
  • Deliverables and accelerators (some are restricted to S-user accounts)

2. SAP Best Practices Explorer

This portal hosts solution-specific best practices, including content for SuccessFactors Onboarding.

Access steps:

  • Go to: https://rapid.sap.com/bp/
  • Search: "SuccessFactors Onboarding"

Look for the package:

  • "SAP Best Practices for SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding (3ON)"

Note: Most content here requires an SAP S-user ID.

3. SAP Learning Hub (for Customers and Partners)

If you have access to SAP Learning Hub (professional or partner edition), you can find:

  • Full implementation guides
  • Recorded sessions
  • Sample project plans and configurations
  • Integration guidance (e.g., with Employee Central, Docusign, Document Center, BPE)

Best regards

entry.title: Re: user wants invoices of certain company codes should n...
entry.content:
Hello

To prevent invoices from certain company codes from appearing in MRBR, you have two main options:

Option 1: Avoid blocking the invoices at the source (recommended)

  1. T-code: OMR6 – Set tolerance limits for invoice verification

    • Maintain high enough tolerance values for:
      • PP (price variance)
      • DQ (quantity variance)
    • This prevents blocking during MIRO, meaning invoices won’t be listed in MRBR.

  2. T-code: OMR4 – Maintain blocking reason per company code/vendor

    • Check if blocking reasons (e.g., quantity block, price block) are active for these company codes.
    • You can remove or adjust blocking keys to avoid blocks being set.

Option 2: Exclude invoices in MRBR selection (workaround)

  1. Use selection criteria in MRBR to exclude the company codes manually.

    • Save a variant that always excludes those company codes.

  2. Use a user exit or BAdI to programmatically exclude invoices from specific company codes in MRBR.

    • Relevant enhancement options:
      • BAdI: MRM_BLOCKED_INVOICE
      • Program enhancement: RM08REB_NET (MRBR report)

Important Notes:

  • Excluding company codes manually in the MRBR selection screen is not sufficient unless enforced via a technical enhancement.
  • The cleanest solution is to prevent invoices from being blocked by using appropriate tolerance settings 

Br

entry.title: Re: Regarding the scope of foreign currency valuation for...
entry.content:
Hello @yahaha 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does not allow exclusion from foreign currency valuation based on the order exchange rate (Exch. Rate Acc.) field alone.

This field is used at the sales order level to carry a manual FX rate into pricing and accounting but is not evaluated by the foreign currency valuation process during period-end closing.

1st option, use Valuation Method Rules (SSCUI 102587)

You can define valuation methods that exclude documents using:

  • Document type
  • Reference field
  • Assignment field
  • Custom indicator

This way, if you assign a specific reference or document type to FX-hedged transactions, you can exclude them from valuation.

2nd option: Use a Custom Field or Tag

  • Use Custom Fields and Logic to mark transactions with a manually entered FX rate.
  • This won’t affect valuation run scope directly, but you can:
  • Report/filter such transactions
  • Manually reverse valuation postings, if needed

Not Possible:

  • There is no setting to evaluate the manually entered exchange rate (e.g., Exch. Rate Acc. from VA01) as a condition for valuation exclusion.
  • No BAdI is available in Public Cloud to override valuation selection logic

SAP helpful references

 SAP KBA 3547486: Valuation Applies to All Journal Entries

3041703 - SAP RU-FI: How to exclude reversed and reversing docs from Intercompany Balance Reconciliation

Thank you, and kind regards

Chuma

Chuma

entry.title: Re: Records Merge to one xml based on condition after spl...
entry.content:
Use the below groovy to group and then use the splitter

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def inputXml = message.getBody(String)
    def parser = new XmlSlurper().parseText(inputXml)

    // Group <row> elements by <Country> value
    def grouped = parser.row.groupBy { it.Country.text() }

    // Build output XML: each <Data> contains all <row> with the same <Country>
    def writer = new StringWriter()
    def builder = new MarkupBuilder(writer)
    builder.root {
        grouped.each { country, rows ->
            Data {
                rows.each { rowNode ->
                    builder.row {
                        rowNode.children().each { field ->
                            builder."${field.name()}"(field.text())
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    message.setBody(writer.toString())
    return message
}

If you want you can use XSLT also

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet version="3.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">
	<xsl:output method="xml" indent="yes"/>
	<xsl:template match="/root">
		<root>
			<xsl:for-each-group select="row" group-by="Country">
				<Data>
					<xsl:for-each select="current-group()">
						<row>
							<xsl:copy-of select="*"/>
						</row>
					</xsl:for-each>
				</Data>
			</xsl:for-each-group>
		</root>
	</xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>

entry.title: Re: Confirm parent order when child order is confirmed
entry.content:
Hello

 

Yes, your requirement can be fulfilled using phantom assemblies in SAP Production Planning (PP) and Material Requirements Planning (MRP), with the correct BOM and order management setup.

 

 

---

 

Requirement Recap:

 

Top-level material should act as a phantom assembly (not inventoried).

 

The child level should be a stocked/inventoried item.

 

You want the child production order to be confirmed, and the phantom parent to be auto-confirmed, but only the child should generate inventory.

 

 

 

---

 

Recommended Solution Using Phantom Assembly:

 

1. Material Master for Phantom Material (Top-Level):

 

Go to MM02 (Change Material).

 

View: MRP 2

 

Field: Special Procurement Type = 50 (Phantom Assembly)

 

MRP Type: Should allow BOM explosion (e.g., PD)

 

 

2. BOM Setup:

 

Create a BOM for the phantom parent material.

 

Include the child material as a component.

 

When you run MRP or create a planned order for the finished product that includes the phantom, the system will skip creating an order for the phantom and instead create orders for its components.

 

 

3. Routing and Order Setup:

 

Ensure the phantom assembly has no routing.

 

The child material (which is inventoried) should have its own production version, BOM, and routing.

 

 

4. Order Behavior:

 

When you run MRP:

 

No planned or production order will be created for the phantom.

 

A production order is created for the child material.

 

 

Once the child order is confirmed, the phantom is implicitly treated as confirmed (since it doesn’t physically exist).

 

 

5. Inventory Posting:

 

Inventory will be posted only for the child material.

 

The phantom assembly will not generate stock or inventory postings.

 

 

 

---

 

Key Points Summary:

 

Object Configuration

 

Phantom Material Special Procurement Type = 50 (Phantom Assembly)

BOM Phantom as parent; child is a stocked component

Routing No routing for phantom; child has own routing if needed

Order Confirmation Confirm child production order; no order exists for phantom

Inventory Behavior Inventory is posted only for child, not for phantom

 

 

 

---

 

Optional Enhancement (if you use PP-PI or advanced PP): If you need more control or automation (e.g., auto-confirming a logical structure), you could use co-product/by-product structures or custom BAdIs to simulate parent confirmation based on child order status. But in most discrete manufacturing cases, the phantom assembly logic is sufficient and standard.

entry.title: Re: Able to drop column or UDF in SAP business one
entry.content:
This is an old thread but i have a different opinion:

Yes you can remove UDF fields from "Tools-Customization tools" weather empty or not, it's your choice, and the table structure at database level actually changes, it's not that just hides them or whatever, it indeed removes the columns.

Weather it frees disk space or not  depends on the actual amount of space used by those columns and the data contained, but at the end you might want to shrink the database files so it reclaims actual disk space, because DBMSs reserve disk space for future growth and never frees it back unless you say so.

I've done this several times, specially when cleaning up old UDF that are no longer needed.

entry.title: Rise into the Future with Agentic AI in Enterprise Asset Management
entry.content:
In 2025, "Agentic AI" has emerged as a transformative force in the world of Business. But what exactly does this buzzword mean for businesses striving to optimize their asset maintenance and operational efficiency?

The upcoming, "Rise into the Future: Expert Demo Session – SAP AI Agents in Action" hosted by SAP, offers a compelling glimpse into how agentic AI is revolutionizing asset management within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. You will explore the practical benefits and innovations that agentic AI brings to the table for enterprises looking to streamline their maintenance processes.

What is Agentic AI?

Agentic AI refers to intelligent AI agents that act autonomously within a business context to solve complex problems and execute tasks with minimal human intervention. Unlike traditional AI, which often requires manual inputs and oversight, agentic AI understands the broader business environment and can make decisions that align with organizational goals. This capability is especially powerful in asset management, where coordination between production schedules, resources, and maintenance plans is critical.

Transforming Enterprise Asset Maintenance

The integration of agentic AI into SAP’s cloud ERP system is designed to bring clarity, speed, and control to enterprise asset maintenance. Here’s how:

  • Alignment of Production and Maintenance: AI agents dynamically synchronize production schedules with maintenance activities, ensuring that asset upkeep does not disrupt operations. This alignment reduces downtime and maximizes asset availability.
  • Resource Optimization: By understanding resource availability and constraints, AI agents allocate maintenance tasks efficiently, preventing bottlenecks and overuse of personnel or equipment.
  • Simplified Task Management: The AI copilot feature breaks down complex maintenance tasks into clear, actionable steps. This simplification empowers maintenance teams to execute their work more effectively and with greater confidence.

To Learn More

The "Rise into the Future: Expert Demo Session" by SAP is your opportunity to see agentic AI in action. Experts will demonstrate how these AI agents work seamlessly within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to transform enterprise asset management. Attendees will gain insights into how AI-driven workflows can help their organizations achieve operational excellence and maintain a competitive edge.

Click here to register.

entry.title: Re: Custom Adapter Module: Can I Deploy Third-Party Depen...
entry.content:
GSON is a serialization/deserialization jar, should not have a security issue. 

But always the rule is use what is present as standard and extend it it required, there should be some trace in the deployment logs to indicate root cause of the error. 

There are already existing api`s for mail and serialization/de-serialiation and json handling and security, adding these new jars changes and/or affects existing standard functionality, hence they would not be allowed. 

Can you share what were the additional features being added for which you needed these extra jars, there might be other ways to get that functionality using exiting jars, that would be the way to go, i have built custom sftp adapters on PI long ago with added jars having no issue, so this made me wonder. In all probability, no one will reply here :). 

Regards

Ravi

entry.title: Email Notification in BPA
entry.content:
Hi everyone.

I have a question and would like your help.

We are currently testing BPA with the goal of automating processes (workflow).

This process was created in BPA and is made available to users through Workzone.

However, email notifications are not sent to users, informing them that new tasks are available for execution.

The only way I know to send an email is by creating a specific task to send it. But I would have to create many tasks for this. Example: I have 10 tasks in a given process, I will have to create 10 more just to send the email. Not to mention that I will not be able to include the link to access the task in the body of the email.

I would like to know if BPA has any automatic way of sending emails to the responsible users without having to create specific tasks for sending emails ("email task").

entry.title: Re: Formatting DateTime Columns in Story Analytics (Repor...
entry.content:
SAP came up w this KBA: 2986093 - How to manually change the Date Format for a date field in Story report - SAP for Me

In my view, this is a deficiency on the product to allow clients via UI to pick the format for Date/Time fields

the version that worked for me was:

MM/dd/yy
CONCAT(TOTEXT(MONTH([FIELD_NAME])),CONCAT("/",CONCAT(TOTEXT(DAY([FIELD_NAME])),CONCAT("/", RIGHT(TOTEXT(YEAR([FIELD_NAME])),2)))))

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Migration Cockpit: Batches with internal numbering are not possible?
entry.content:
Hello Torsten,

there is also a KBA regarding the limited length of batch numbers.

3083930 - Length of batch number is limited to 10 characters in SAP S/4HANA Cloud - SAP for Me

The solution within that KBA is to use a new characteristic field containing the longer batch number. That characteristics should then be attached to  batch class that will then be assigned to the batch master.

The data migration object can only support what the used function module offers.  As the function module only offers external numbering, it is not possible to implement internal numbering within the data migration object. 

BR

Frank

entry.title: Identifying SAP Certificates in Personal Security Environment: A Comprehensive Guide
entry.content:
Understanding Personal Security Environment (PSE) Fundamentals

At its core, a Personal Security Environent (PSE) in SAP is a digital container designed to securely store cryptographic information, primarily digital certificates and their corresponding private keys. Think of it as a digital wallet for your SAP system's security credentials. PSEs are essential for enabling secure communication channels, such as those used in SSL/TLS, SNC (Secure Network Communications), and digital signatures.

There are several types of PSEs, each serving a specific purpose within the SAP ecosystem:

  • System PSE (SSFS): Often used for internal system-to-system communication and secure storage of sensitive data like database credentials.
  • Application PSE: Employed by specific SAP applications for their secure communication needs.
  • Client PSE: Used by SAP clients (e.g., SAP GUI, external applications) to authenticate themselves to SAP servers.

PSEs can also exist in different formats, primarily:

  • Legacy format (v2): An older format that might still be found in some older SAP installations.
  • Current format (v4): The modern and more secure format, offering enhanced cryptographic capabilities.

The Structure and Components of a PSE

To effectively identify certificates within a PSE, it's crucial to understand its internal structure and the components it typically contains:

  • Owner Certificate (with Private Key): This is the primary certificate associated with the PSE, along with its unique private key. This pair is used by the SAP system or application to identify itself and encrypt/decrypt data.
  • Certificate Chain: A sequence of certificates that links the owner certificate back to a trusted root Certificate Authority (CA). This chain establishes the trustworthiness of the owner certificate.
  • Trust Anchors (Trusted Certificates): These are certificates of trusted Certificate Authorities (CAs) or self-signed certificates from other systems that the SAP system explicitly trusts. They are used to validate the authenticity of incoming certificates.
  • Address Book (Optional): In some cases, a PSE might also contain an address book of trusted communication partners.

PSE files are typically stored in the file system, often with a .pse extension. Common storage locations include the sec directory under the SAP instance directory (e.g., /usr/sap/<SID>/<Instance>/sec/). For certificates managed within the SAP HANA database, they might reside in database tables such as SYS.CERTIFICATES and SYS.PSE_CERTIFICATES.

To protect the sensitive private keys and certificates, PSEs employ several protection mechanisms:

  • PIN Protection: A password or PIN is often used to encrypt the private key within the PSE, preventing unauthorized access.
  • File Permissions: Operating system file permissions are critical to restrict access to the PSE files themselves, ensuring only authorized users or processes can read or modify them.
  • Database Privileges: For in-database certificates, appropriate database privileges are essential to control who can view, modify, or delete certificate entries.

Dissecting Certificate Structure for Identification:

Before diving into identification methods, let's briefly review the key elements of a digital certificate that aid in its identification:

Distinguished Name (DN) Components: The DN is a unique identifier for the certificate's subject. Key components include:

  • Common Name (CN): Typically the hostname or application name.
  • Organization (O): The name of the organization.
  • Organizational Unit (OU): A specific department or unit within the organization.
  • Country (C): The two-letter country code.

Key Certificate Attributes: Beyond the DN, other attributes provide crucial identification details:

  • Serial Number: A unique identifier assigned by the Certificate Authority.
  • Validity Period: The start and end dates during which the certificate is considered valid.
  • Issuer Information: Details about the Certificate Authority that issued the certificate.
  • Key Usage: Defines the cryptographic purposes for which the public key contained in the certificate can be used (e.g., digital signature, key encipherment).
  • Signature Algorithm: The algorithm used to sign the certificate.

Methods for Certificate Identification

Identifying certificates in SAP PSEs can be approached through various methods, each with its own strengths and use cases.

1. Identification by Location

The simplest form of identification is by knowing where PSE files are typically stored. Standard file paths and instance-specific locations are common starting points. For SAP HANA, certificates might also be found within database collections.

2. Identification by Attributes

Once a certificate is accessed, its attributes can be used for precise identification:

  • Distinguished Name (DN) Matching: Comparing the DN components against expected values.
  • Serial Number Lookup: Searching for a specific certificate by its unique serial number.
  • Fingerprint Comparison: Using cryptographic hash functions (e.g., SHA-256) to generate a unique fingerprint of the certificate for comparison.

3. Identification by Purpose

Certificates are often used for specific functions, which can also aid in their identification:

  • SSL Server Certificates: Used by SAP servers to secure incoming connections.
  • SSL Client Certificates: Used by SAP clients to authenticate to servers.
  • SAML Certificates: Used for Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) based single sign-on.
  • Signing Certificates: Used for digital signatures.

Tools for Certificate Identification

SAP provides several tools, both command-line and GUI-based, to help with certificate identification and management.

Using the sapgenpse Tool

sapgenpse is a powerful command-line utility provided by SAP for managing PSEs and certificates. It's particularly useful for scripting and automated tasks.

Key commands for identification include:

  • sapgenpse get_my_cert -p <PSE_path> -v: Extracts and displays the owner certificate from a specified PSE file.
  • sapgenpse maintain_pk -p <PSE_path> -l: Lists all certificates contained within a PSE file.
  • sapgenpse maintain_ssl_db: Used for managing the SSL trust store, which can also reveal certificate details.

The output of these commands provides detailed certificate information, including validity status and trust chain information.

Using the STRUST Transaction

For those who prefer a graphical interface, the STRUST transaction in SAP GUI is the primary tool for managing PSEs and certificates. It offers a user-friendly way to visualize and interact with certificate data.

Navigate to STRUST via the transaction code. Here, you can select and manage various PSE types. STRUST provides a visual certificate browser, allowing you to view detailed information about each certificate, including its chain, validity, and key attributes. It also supports import and export functionalities.

Python-Based Identification

For advanced automation and integration with other systems, Python scripting offers a flexible and powerful approach to certificate identification. Libraries like cryptography or OpenSSL can be used to parse certificate files, while custom scripts can be developed to interact with SAP systems or database views.

Python-based solutions offer significant automation benefits, including bulk certificate discovery, automated metadata extraction, and proactive expiration monitoring.

Best Practices for SAP Certificate Management

Effective certificate identification is just one piece of the puzzle. Implementing best practices ensures a secure and manageable SAP certificate landscape.

  • Naming Conventions: Adopt consistent Distinguished Name (DN) formats and use purpose-indicating Common Names (CNs). Include version tracking in comments or metadata.
  • Organization: Centralize PSE management where possible. Maintain comprehensive documentation of certificate purposes and regularly update your certificate inventory.
  • Monitoring: Implement automated expiration checks to prevent outages. Regularly validate trust chains and audit certificate usage to detect anomalies.

Common Challenges and Solutions

Managing SAP certificates comes with its share of challenges. Here are some common ones and their practical solutions:

Challenge: Identifying certificates across distributed systems.

  • Solution: Implement a centralized certificate inventory system with location mapping to track all PSEs and certificates across your SAP landscape.

Challenge: Determining certificate purpose.

  • Solution: Enforce standardized naming conventions and metadata tagging during certificate creation to clearly indicate their intended use. 

Challenge: Legacy format detection.

  • Solution: Utilize version-aware tools (like sapgenpse with appropriate flags) and plan for systematic conversion of legacy PSEs to current formats.

Challenge: PIN-protected PSEs.

  • Solution: Implement secure PIN management strategies, potentially leveraging secure credential stores or automation tools that can securely provide PINs when needed.

Conclusion

Identifying SAP certificates within the Personal Security Environment is a critical aspect of maintaining a secure and reliable SAP landscape. It requires a systematic approach, leveraging a combination of SAP-provided tools like sapgenpse and STRUST, alongside powerful scripting capabilities offered by Python.

By understanding the fundamentals of PSE, its structure, and the various identification methods, you can gain better control over your SAP security infrastructure. Adhering to best practices in naming, organization, and monitoring, coupled with proactive solutions to common challenges, will significantly enhance your ability to manage certificates effectively and prevent security vulnerabilities or system outages.

Ultimately, a comprehensive and up-to-date inventory of your SAP certificates forms the foundation for robust security and efficient operations. Embrace these strategies to ensure your SAP systems remain secure and trustworthy.

entry.title: BOM Design for Routing-Based Subcontracting with Variable Process Loss (Fasteners Manuf. Comp)
entry.content:
I am currently implementing a scenario for a fastener manufacturing company involving a routing-based subcontracting process.

In this process, the purchase order unit is Each (EA), while the pricing unit is Kilogram (KG). For every subcontracting PO, the finished product quantity remains constant in EA, but the actual received weight in KG varies depending on the processing outcome.

The client records the process loss as the difference between the issued component weight and the received finished product weight, both in KG.

🔹 Example:

1st PO

  • Order Unit: EA

  • Price Unit: KG

  • Weight per Unit: 1.69 grams

  • Issued Quantity: 10,000 EA = 16.9 KG

  • Received Quantity: 10,000 EA = 15.9 KG

  • Process Loss: 1 KG

2nd PO

  • Order Unit: EA

  • Price Unit: KG

  • Weight per Unit: 1.69 grams

  • Issued Quantity: 10,000 EA = 16.9 KG

  • Received Quantity: 10,000 EA = 16.1 KG

  • Process Loss: 0.8 KG

🔹 Requirement:

Since the process loss varies with each PO, how can we define the BOM to support this scenario while ensuring proper component consumption tracking and accurate cost posting?

entry.title: Re: Email Notification in BPA
entry.content:
Hello Fabio

By default, SAP BPA does not automatically send email notifications for user tasks unless certain configurations are in place. Here’s how to handle this without creating one email task per process step:

1. Enable Email Notifications in SAP BTP Subaccount Settings

You need to configure and enable workflow email notifications in your BTP subaccount where SAP Build Process Automation is running.

Steps:

  • Go to BTP Cockpit
  • Navigate to your Subaccount > Service Instances > Find the Workflow or Process Automation instance
  • Under Destinations, ensure that mail notification settings are active
  • Make sure a destination named SAP_NOTIFICATION_CENTER exists and is correctly configured
  • You must also configure the Workflow Mail Destination pointing to your email service provider or SMTP endpoint

2. Activate Email Notifications per User via My Inbox Settings

  • Users need to go to SAP Work Zone or My Inbox
  • In the Settings, activate "Email Notifications"
  • This allows the system to automatically send emails when a task is assigned

This step is user-specific, and must be enabled by each end-user or enforced via role-based setup.

3. Use Business Rules or Automation to Trigger Email Once

If you want more control, you can use a central automation or business rule to:

  • Detect when a task is created
  • Read the context and assigned user
  • Send one email per user per task using one reusable automation

This avoids one email task per user task.

4. Use SAP Alert Notification Service (optional)

If you need structured notifications:

  • Subscribe to SAP Alert Notification service
  • Configure it to trigger alerts on specific BPA events (like task assignment)
  • Send email or push alerts via defined templates

5. Include Task Link in Email Body (Optional)

SAP BPA doesn't include direct task links automatically. To include the task link in a custom email:

  • Use the following format: https:///inbox?instanceId=
  • You can retrieve the instance ID and task ID via automation or workflow context
  • Use that in your email content dynamically via scripting

entry.title: Re: Revenue & COGS elimination in Group Reporting
entry.content:
This is a common scenario: having multiple COGS accounts in the Chart of Accounts (CoA).

You can include all the COGS accounts (FS items) in the Sequence 20 selection. Add the PU in the selection using the PU hierarchy.

During the GL Account vs. FS Item mapping, you can map all source COGS accounts to a single global or group-level COGS account. (Note: This approach is not recommended.)

entry.title: Re: How to Skip One Branch After the Other Completes in S...
entry.content:
Hi

Step-by-Step Solution

  1. Start the process with an API trigger
    You’ve already got this part – perfect.

  2. Create a parallel branch in the process
    Set up two parallel paths after the trigger:

  • Branch A: Approval form sent to user
  • Branch B: Wait for external API call
  1. Introduce a Decision Variable (e.g. winner)
  • Create a process-level variable named "winner" (string or boolean)
  • Default: empty or false
  1. In Branch A (Approval Form):
  • After the user submits the approval form:
    • Add a Script Task or Automation Step that:
      • Checks if winner is empty
      • If yes: sets winner = A
      • If no: do nothing (means B already completed)
  • Add an Exclusive Gateway:
    • If winner == A: continue process
    • If winner != A: end or skip the rest of the branch
  1. In Branch B (Wait for API Event):
  • Use a Wait for Event trigger (intermediate message event)
  • Once triggered:
    • Add a Script Task that:
      • Checks if winner is empty
      • If yes: sets winner = B
      • If no: do nothing
  • Add an Exclusive Gateway:
    • If winner == B: continue process
    • If winner != B: end or skip the rest of the branch

Logic Recap

  • Both branches run in parallel.
  • The first to complete sets the shared variable winner.
  • The other branch checks this flag before continuing.
  • If the flag doesn't match the current branch, that path ends silently.

Tips

  • You can use Automation Step with condition and update logic if Script Task is not available.
  • This works best when the user form and API response are independent.
  • Make sure that the variable winner is set atomically — BPA handles process variables safely within one process instance.

Example Use Case

Trigger: API call
Parallel Tasks: Show form / Wait API callback
Condition Control: First to set winner
Result: Other branch ends

entry.title: How Smart Maintenance Is Changing the Game – A Journey Through Innovation with SAP
entry.content:
Telling a story of a case that happens repeatedly and often makes us wonder, ‘what was missing this time?’. It was a huge operation in Germany—rows of heavy machinery humming in perfect rhythm. Yet every few weeks, production would grind to a halt. A pump would fail. A belt would snap. A sensor would go silent.

The irony? They had the data. Sensors were everywhere. But the systems weren’t connected. The insights weren’t clear. The maintenance strategy was reactive.

You already know SAP Intelligent Asset Management ?

Came hear some of these examples at SAP Inside Track Porto 2025.

From Chaos to Control: The Power of Prediction

Their journey started with a simple question:
What if we could predict failures before they happen?

With SAP Predictive Asset Insights, they began feeding real-time sensor data into machine learning models. Over time, the system learned to recognize early warning signs, detect anomalies, and trigger alerts before a failure occurred.

Next, they implemented SAP Asset Strategy and Performance Management, defining smarter, risk-based maintenance strategies. What once was guesswork became a structured, data-informed plan.

And suddenly, downtime dropped. Efficiency climbed. The maintenance team stopped reacting and started anticipating.

A Network of Intelligence

But the real breakthrough came when they connected to the SAP Asset Intelligence Network. Now, OEMs, service providers, and plant operators could instantly share digital twins, manuals, and updates.

Imagine a technician in the field, standing in front of a faulty machine. With a tablet and SAP Mobile Asset Management, they could access the exact service history, recommended fix, and even a video from the manufacturer — all in seconds.

This wasn’t just smart maintenance. This was collaborative intelligence.

Real Stories Across Europe

And this isn’t just one plant. Across Europe, companies are rethinking asset management:

  • In France, utility giant Veolia uses SAP to monitor their vast water infrastructure in real time, improving service reliability while saving millions in emergency repairs.
  • In Germany, Deutsche Bahn monitors thousands of rail components, improving punctuality and reducing disruptions — all thanks to predictive insights.
  • In the Netherlands, energy provider Eneco uses IoT-enabled maintenance to keep wind turbines spinning, increasing uptime and boosting renewable energy output.

These aren’t tech demos. They’re business revolutions.

A New Era of Asset Management

What’s most exciting? This is just the beginning.

With artificial intelligence, IoT, and SAP S/4HANA, asset management is evolving into something far more powerful - a strategic pillar of resilience, performance, and sustainability.

Companies that embrace this transformation are no longer just fixing machines.
They are unlocking the full potential of their operations.

And the plant in Germany? It’s no longer silent from unexpected failures.
It’s running smarter, longer, and better than ever.

Tiago Fernandes  emphasized the importance of industry-standard data and taxonomy by partnering with Reliability Dynamics:

“Better decision‑making requires better Asset Management information, and better information requires better data. SAP & Reliability Dynamics provide an integrated tool to standardize Extended ISO 14224 taxonomy … failure catalogs … equipment characteristics.”  This reinforces the need for clean, structured data to power effective IAM implementations.

You can participate in this and other topics and listen to insights at SAP Inside Track Porto, where Tiago Fernandes  will speak.

References Veolia Case

  • Integration of asset and finance systems, mobile field data, and procurement directly in EAM/SAP teamwork.net+2istart.co.nz+2anz.veolia.com+2community.sap.com
  • Use of Hubgrade Blockage Prediction with machine learning to optimize pipeline investment watertechnologies.com+2anz.veolia.com+2veolia.com+2
  • SVG digital tools for leak detection, crisis management, and real-time plant insights watertechnologies.com+2veolia.com+2watertechnologies.com+2

entry.title: Re: SAP MRM: What is the retention period of the job logs...
entry.content:
Hello,

The Request Current Market Data job executed via Schedule Treasury Back Office Jobs follows the standard retention settings of the SAP job management framework. Here's how it works:

Job Log Retention

  • The default retention period for job logs in the SAP system is typically 30 days.
  • This applies to all jobs executed via the job scheduler, including Treasury jobs, unless customized.
  • The cleanup is usually handled by background programs such as RSBTCDEL.

Spool Retention

  • Spool requests are handled by the TemSe system and follow a separate retention policy.
  • The default spool retention is usually 8 days, unless modified in transaction SPAD or through jobs like RSPO1041 (Spool deletion).

Recommendation

To ensure compliance and clarity:

  1. Ask your BASIS team to review job log retention via RSBTCDEL settings.
  2. Confirm the spool retention policy in SPAD or related deletion jobs.
  3. Adjust the retention periods if your business or audit requirements demand longer storage.

entry.title: Re: ABSENCE QUOTA CARRY OVER
entry.content:
What is the Time Type that allows you to generate an Allowance for Quota 16?  For this exercise, I will assume that it is Q16A. 

Create an Information Time Type to carry the Quota's Balance (such as Q15B).

Create a PCR to read the Balance of Quota 15 on the Last Day of the Quota's Validation Period, using Operation OUTAQTYP to query Quota 15, Operation HRS=QCREST to query it's balance, and then Operation ADDDB Q15B, and call that PCR through Function P2006.

Create an other PCR to query "Yesterday's" value of Time Type Q16B on the First Day of the Quota's Validation Period, using Operation HRS=LQ16B followed by ADDDBQ16A.

entry.title: Re: Custom CBC Customizations
entry.content:
Hello @vladimir_culic 

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, your requirement to maintain and transport a custom mapping (external SAP company codes) is a common scenario, but with specific limitations

 Key Points

  • Custom Business Objects (CBOs)
    • Can be created and exposed via API.
    • Data is not transportable across systems, only structure moves.
    • This is standard behaviour in the Public Cloud.
  • Custom configuration tables:
  • Created via key-user extensibility (e.g. Custom Tables in Custom Fields and Logic).
  • Data is transportable via customised transports (CBC).
  • But they are not API-exposable by default.
  • Activate Business Event Logging and annotations like @ObjectModel.configuration don’t apply here, they are not supported for this use case in Public Cloud.

SAP Help Custom Business Objects

SAP Recommendation

  • For API access → use CBO, maintain data separately per tenant.
  • For transportable data → use Customising Table (UI only).
  • For advanced control, → manage mapping externally via SAP BTP (CAP service).

Let me know if you need further information

With kind regards

Chuma

Crystal Report Version :  2010

I already have the SQL Select statement to get the data, now, in order to filter the data using two Dates as a Date range, I need to create the Starting Date and End Date, I started doing that.  Then, I tried to use those two parameters in the COMMAND file I created, but is not working, it means all the data is passing. thru.

Those two parameters,  I need to use as the End Date : the Current Date, I created as p_CurrentDate.

And for the Beginning Date, I need to use a calculated value as :  The first Parameter minus 30 days,  it means  :  f_CurrentDate - 30 Days.

These  should be taken by default, to be run by SAP BI and creating an excel file.

Then.

First Paremeter :  p_CurrentDate :  Taking the CurrentDate from the System

Second Parameter :  p_CurrentDate - 30 Days.Date,

But, the user, also, could run the Crystal report to get a different period of time, it means the CurrentDate would be different, because it becomes the end date, then, the Begin Date sould be equeally 30 days before.

So, the first parameter ( p_CurrentDate ), should be flexible to get the really Current Date or to use a different date to caluclate the second parameter, maybe using a formula.

So, for some reason, when I run the report, is not taking the two parameters to filter the data and show only the records in that Date range.

May I missing something in the parameters Settings?

I attached a file with the setup of that Parameter, the second one is very similar.

entry.title: [SAP Build Process Automation] Desktop Agent does not synchronize deployed projects and stuck logs
entry.content:
Dear SAP Build Process Automation Community:

I'm having a persistent and very frustrating issue with my SAP Build Process Automation desktop agent. It doesn't seem to sync deployed projects despite seemingly correct configuration and several troubleshooting attempts. I would greatly appreciate any help, suggestions, or diagnostics based on your experiences.SAP Build Process Automation 

 

The core problem (with the desktop agent!):

My Desktop Agent (v3.37.57 on Windows 11) constantly displays the message "It appears no projects have been received yet" in the "Projects" window. Most worryingly, its local logs ("History/Events") are completely blocked, showing the last activity as [Exact date of capture, e.g., June 9, 2025], even though it is [Current date, e.g., June 18, 2025].

This indicates that the agent is not processing or updating its status locally, nor is it downloading projects that have been deployed.

 

Project context and configuration (Everything looks fine in the cloud!):

I've set up a project to automate the mass update of fixed asset master records in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The cloud configuration appears to be correct:

Project: Mass Update Asset Master Data (57O).
Cloud Status: Deployed and started in RPADeveloper environment (confirmed in "Environments" > "Projects").
Automation Launcher: The Mass Update Asset Master Data (57O) artifact (Automation Launcher type) is configured with the main automation (MassUpdateAssetMasterData) in the "Start Manually from Agent" section.
Agent Connection to Cloud:
In the BTP lobby (Environments > RPADeveloper > Agent Management), my agent (NDTSCZ1166) appears as "Inactive" and "Connected".
In the agent's local settings (Configuration > Tenants), the connection to efdadad3trial.us10.process-automation.build.cloud.sap is "Active".

entry.title: How to display RF Message in a separate Screen - RF Screens with Touch Enablement
entry.content:
In EWM standard RF transactions, the following standard function codes are only available as function key but not as pushbutton prior to S4 HANA 2020

  • Clear input field value ('CLEAR') - F6
  • Return to previous step ('BACK') - F7
  • Display value list of actual field ('LIST') - F8
  • Display message on separate screen ('FULLMS') - F9

Example: If an error message is triggered during execution of a particular RF transaction( I.e Receiving HU by HU MO), the message is partially displayed in current session and to display full message, it is required to press 'F9' to display the full message and then press 'F7' to return to the previous Screen.

On devices without hardware function keys, these function codes either must be triggered via a software keypad or by entering the corresponding value in the shortcut field on the RF device. Both possibilities are not user-friendly and decrease the performance of the RF use

Solution: From S4 HANA 2020 onwards, SAP has released a note '2957972 - RF Framework: Add Standard Function Codes as Pushbuttons which provides to add F6, F7, F8 and F9 as an easy Push button in RF framework and easy navigation of message in a separate screen w/o usage of pressing these in RF Device.

In /SCWM/PRDVC, presentation profile, SAP has given 4 different attribute to show 'Functions as Push Button to be used based on User UI acceptability.

To use this functionality, customer have to adjust their 'Display Profile' in customizing. To display the function( F6 to F9) as push button along with 'Message display on a Pop-up message Screen' customer has to configure display profile in customizing in RF.

Extended Warehouse Management-->Mobile Data Entry-->Radio Frequency (RF) Framework-->RF Screen Manager. ( Avoid changing in standard **( 8*40) used for mounted screen on forklift  & *2 Portrait Mode( 15*26) generally used for Handheld devices.

Rather copy ** or *2 in your project requirement.

Change here Message display from '1' to '0'. Standard has be default as 1

Ensure to check the Screen manager has correct 'display message'

Good to test once above settings are performed.

Check /SCWM/RFUI in Fiori as well as in ITS( In this example, I am not using MoBgui). The required standard function codes are only available as an easy Push Button:)

Lets test any RF transaction to check 'Display Message' in a separate screen along with Std Functions as push button. 

System has added 4 std pushbuttons along with Screen specific RF pushbuttons. 

Also an easy List view is available to select the required work center( in RF REHU by MO transaction)

Displaying message in separate Screen and easy return to Previous screen.

Below example, error message are directly navigated to a separate screen( without touching F9 button) and one Press enter button navigates to original screen without pressing F7 on Handheld.

There is also a BAdI also /SCWM/EX_RF_ADD_FC_AS_PB  which allows to implement a customer own logic how the standard function codes are added to the pushbutton groups. The BAdI also has an example implementation which shows how function codes GTEXCP (to display valid exception codes), BACK and CLEAR are added to the first pushbutton group.

Thanks for reading. All tests have been performed in S4 HANA 2023 FPS02 Sandbox system.

Refences: 

2957972 - RF Framework: Add Standard Function Codes as Pushbuttons

3234853 - Error Message /SCWM/UI_RF301 while adding Buttons in tcode /SCWM/PRDVC

R&D had another look at what you are doing and suggested using this rather that 100%:

If the BestFitPage was set to False, then you had to specify the detailed Width/Height value like below: BestFitPage="false" Height="1200px" ToolPanelView="None" Width="1600px"

entry.title: Re: Custom MDF Analysis Dimension problem - Neuroinclusion
entry.content:
Hi @DariusSoreata - I don't understand why you need to enable External Code from Cust Neuroinclusion object.  This is setup as Primary Person ID so I assume the External Code contain User ID of employee.  But you only enable this column from this object.  For what? 

In Cust Neuroinclusiondetails object, you have External Code setup as Secondary Person ID, that means this field contain Person ID value of employee.  If you already have Secondary Person ID from Cust Neuroinclusiondetails object, why do you need External Code from Cust Neuroinclusion object?  The link between Primary and Secondary Person ID is already available in Employment Info table.  

Maybe try to remove Cust Neuroinclusion object entirely and see if the initial load will process successfully or not.

entry.title: Re: My First RAP Project. Many errors in Child and Parent projection view.
entry.content:
Hi Mio,

Below is my Base entity code.

************Create Interface View****************
 @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
 @EndUserText.label: 'Booking BO view'
 define view entity ZI_BOOKING_g
   as select from zbooking_data as Booking

   association [1..1] to ztravel_data        as _Travel     on  $projection.TravelUUID = _Travel.travel_uuid
   
   association [1..1] to /DMO/I_Customer           as _Customer   on  $projection.CustomerID   = _Customer.CustomerID
   association [1..1] to /DMO/I_Carrier            as _Carrier    on  $projection.CarrierID    = _Carrier.AirlineID
   association [1..1] to /DMO/I_Connection         as _Connection on  $projection.CarrierID    = _Connection.AirlineID
                                                                  and $projection.ConnectionID = _Connection.ConnectionID
   association [1..1] to /DMO/I_Flight             as _Flight     on  $projection.CarrierID    = _Flight.AirlineID
                                                                  and $projection.ConnectionID = _Flight.ConnectionID
                                                                  and $projection.FlightDate   = _Flight.FlightDate
   association [0..1] to I_Currency                as _Currency   on $projection.CurrencyCode    = _Currency.Currency    
 {
   key booking_uuid          as BookingUUID,
       travel_uuid           as TravelUUID,
       booking_id            as BookingID,
       booking_date          as BookingDate,
       customer_id           as CustomerID,
       carrier_id            as CarrierID,
       connection_id         as ConnectionID,
       flight_date           as FlightDate,
       @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
       flight_price          as FlightPrice,
       currency_code         as CurrencyCode,
       @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
       created_by            as CreatedBy,
       @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
       last_changed_by       as LastChangedBy,
       @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
       local_last_changed_at as LocalLastChangedAt,

       /* associations */
       _Travel,
       _Customer,
       _Carrier,
       _Connection,
       _Flight,
       _Currency
 }

*********************************************
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
 @EndUserText.label: 'Travel BO view'
 define view entity ZI_TRAVEL_G
   as select from ztravel_data as Travel

   association [0..*] to zbooking_data as _Booking on $projection.TravelUUID = _Booking.Travel_UUID
   
   association [0..1] to /DMO/I_Agency       as _Agency   on $projection.AgencyID = _Agency.AgencyID
   association [0..1] to /DMO/I_Customer     as _Customer on $projection.CustomerID = _Customer.CustomerID
   association [0..1] to I_Currency          as _Currency on $projection.CurrencyCode = _Currency.Currency  
 {
   key travel_uuid           as TravelUUID,
       travel_id             as TravelID,
       agency_id             as AgencyID,
       customer_id           as CustomerID,
       begin_date            as BeginDate,
       end_date              as EndDate,
       @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
       booking_fee           as BookingFee,
       @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
       total_price           as TotalPrice,
       currency_code         as CurrencyCode,
       description           as Description,
       overall_status        as TravelStatus,
       @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
       created_by            as CreatedBy,
       @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
       created_at            as CreatedAt,
       @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
       last_changed_by       as LastChangedBy,
       @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
       last_changed_at       as LastChangedAt,
       @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
       local_last_changed_at as LocalLastChangedAt,

       /* associations */
       _Booking,
       _Agency,
       _Customer,
       _Currency      
 }

Below is my Projection entity code

 @EndUserText.label: 'Travel BO projection view'
 @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
 @search.searchable: true
 @Metadata.allowExtensions: true

 define root view entity ZC_TRAVEL_G
   as projection on ZI_TRAVEL_G as Travel
 {
   key TravelUUID,
       @search.defaultSearchElement: true
       TravelID,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: '/DMO/I_Agency', element: 'AgencyID'} }]
       @ObjectModel.text.element: ['AgencyName']
       @search.defaultSearchElement: true
       AgencyID,
       _Agency.Name       as AgencyName,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: '/DMO/I_Customer', element: 'CustomerID'} }]
       @ObjectModel.text.element: ['CustomerName']
       @search.defaultSearchElement: true
       CustomerID,
       _Customer.LastName as CustomerName,
       BeginDate,
       EndDate,
       @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
       BookingFee,
       @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
       TotalPrice,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'I_Currency', element: 'Currency'} }]
       CurrencyCode,
       Description,
       TravelStatus,
       LastChangedAt,
       LocalLastChangedAt,

       /* Associations */
       _Agency,
       _Booking : redirected to composition child ZC_BOOKING_G,
       _Currency,
       _Customer   
 }

**********************Child projection Entity***********
@EndUserText.label: 'Booking BO projection view'
 @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
 @search.searchable: true
 @Metadata.allowExtensions: true

 define view entity ZC_BOOKING_G
   as projection on ZI_BOOKING_G as Booking
 {
   key BookingUUID,
       TravelUUID,
       @search.defaultSearchElement: true
       BookingID,
       BookingDate,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity : {name: '/DMO/I_Customer', element: 'CustomerID'  } }]
       @ObjectModel.text.element: ['CustomerName']
       @search.defaultSearchElement: true
       CustomerID,
       _Customer.LastName as CustomerName,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{entity: {name: '/DMO/I_Carrier', element: 'AirlineID' }}]
       @ObjectModel.text.element: ['CarrierName']
       CarrierID,
       _Carrier.Name      as CarrierName,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [ {entity: {name: '/DMO/I_Flight', element: 'ConnectionID'},
                                            additionalBinding: [ { localElement: 'CarrierID',    element: 'AirlineID' },
                                                                 { localElement: 'FlightDate',   element: 'FlightDate',   usage: #RESULT},
                                                                 { localElement: 'FlightPrice',  element: 'Price',        usage: #RESULT },
                                                                 { localElement: 'CurrencyCode', element: 'CurrencyCode', usage: #RESULT } ] } ]
       ConnectionID,
       FlightDate,
       @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
       FlightPrice,
       @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{entity: {name: 'I_Currency', element: 'Currency' }}]
       CurrencyCode,
       LocalLastChangedAt,

       /* associations */
       _Travel : redirected to parent ZC_TRAVEL_G,
       _Customer,
       _Carrier,
       _Connection,
       _Flight
 }

This is a code snippet from SAP learning training.

 

Thank you,

Gargi
